prompted_experimental.text: memories flooding back. like a tidal wave. crashing over me. i see it. my childhood home. standing there. unchanged. as if time never passed. or maybe it did. and i forgot. i don't know. can't think. it's like my mind is a puzzle. with missing pieces. and i'm trying to put them back together. but they don't fit. it's there. my home. the one i left. so long ago. i don't know how long. or why. just that i did. and now i'm back. or am i? maybe i never left. maybe i just... forgot. like a dream. that didn't wake up. the windows are the same. the curtains. the porch. everything. it's all... exactly the same. like i just stepped back through time. or into a dream. my dream. i don't know which. i walk up the path. the one i used to run down. so many times. laughing.

The chairs... so many chairs. Like rows of empty tombstones, cold and unyielding. I sit in one, a lonely sentinel in the darkening room. The clock on the wall ticks, its mechanical heartbeat growing slower, more labored. The seconds stretch, a never-ending expanse of nothingness. I think I'm waiting. For something. A name slips from my mind, but it's just out of reach. A person, a face, a... what was it? My head throbs, a dull ache that refuses to subside. The air is heavy, thick with the scent of disinfectant and stale air. The fluorescent lights above flicker, casting eerie shadows on the walls. I try to focus, but my gaze slips away, drawn to the window like a moth to flame. The darkness outside is almost palpable, a living thing that presses against the glass. I wonder if I'm outside, if this is some kind of dream. But

laundry. the machine hums. a steady beat. like my heart. i am in the laundry room. the walls close in. a blur of grey and white. the washer rumbles. it growls. i reach out. my hand wobbly. the detergent dispenser. i open it. powder spills. it falls. a mess. the washer. it's still running. a vortex. the water. it churns. i try to think. to remember. why i'm here. what i'm doing. but my mind. it's foggy. distant. a memory. a song. a melody. it's gone. lost. like sand. slipping through my fingers. the dryer. it's quiet. a cold wind. it blows. through the room. i shiver. my teeth chatter. i try to reach. the controls. but my hand. it's numb. a puppet. on strings. pulled by an invisible force. i see things. distorted. like a funhouse mirror. the washer. it's a giant. looming over me. the dryer. a monster.

foggy light... orange and grey, the world around me... blur. I... wait. for... something. a bus. i think. used to be a bus, always the bus. now it's... a... thing. with wheels. that moves. i think. was i working? i used to work. now i just... am. here. in this... place. with the... pavement. it's wet. my feet are wet. where's my phone? i had my phone. did i leave it at work? no, i didn't leave it at work. or did i? did i take it with me? i don't remember. don't remember anything. people walk by. they're... blurry. faces. too many faces. i try to focus. but they're all... wrong. or am i wrong? i'm the one who's wrong. i'm the one who's... broken. bus... bus... i think i see a bus. no, it's just a... a truck. with a... a trailer. is that a bus? why can't

The house loomed, a monolith in the smoggy haze. It stood frozen, a snapshot of a bygone era. I stepped through the cracked sidewalk, the roughness a whisper against my soles. Memories seeped up, like dampness through a cracked wall. The front door creaked open, hinges screaming in protest. A shiver danced down my spine as I pushed inside. Dust motes swirled, a miniature whirlwind. Time had suspended itself within these walls. The furniture, a relic of a bygone era, remained intact. My childhood bedroom, a shrine to nostalgia, lay before me. The bed, once a sanctuary, now a relic of innocence. The closet door, ajar, as if waiting for me to return. Fragments of memories burst forth, shards of a shattered mirror. My mother's laughter, a melodic whisper. My father's voice, a low hum. The creak of the stairs, a Morse code of forgotten days. I wandered, a ghost

the fluorescent lights overhead cast a sickly glow the waiting room a cavernous space filled with rows of chairs like skeletal fingers reaching up towards the ceiling. she sits alone in a chair towards the back the walls closing in on her the smell of disinfectant and stale air clinging to her skin like a damp shroud. time loses all meaning in this sterile space where the only sound is the gentle hum of the machines beeping out their coded rhythms in the distance a nurse floats past her eyes red-rimmed from lack of sleep her expression a mask of practiced calm. in the corner a TV flickers to life casting a gaudy glow on the walls news anchors mouthing words that mean nothing to her their faces a blur of concern and importance. she stares at the clock on the wall its numbers ticking away with mechanical precision but

In the dimly lit laundry room a drone hums softly its fluorescent light casting an eerie glow on the rows of stacked washing machines the doors ajar like mouths agape awaiting the offering of dirty clothes a tangled mess of fabrics and sweat and detergent the air thick with the stench of mildew and dampness she stands at the sink her hands submerged in warm water as she scrubs at a stubborn stain on a white t-shirt the threads beginning to fray like the frayed edges of her own sanity the world outside the window a blur of concrete and steel and glass a never-ending tapestry of grey and brown and beige the sounds of the city a distant mumble a constant hum a buzzing in her ears in the next stall over a woman's voice rises in a crescendo of despair her words indistinguishable but the anguish palpable like

The bus station a geometry of grey and steel. her footfalls echo off the walls as she waits for the 87. her eyes a blur of exhaustion the fluorescent lights above pulsing like a sick heartbeat the neon glow of the liquor store across the street a pitiful attempt to revitalize the dull atmosphere. the wind stirs the trash that lines the sidewalk and it swirls around her ankles like a maddening dance. she kicks it away with a swift motion her gaze drifting to the schedules posted on the kiosk. the times blur together a seemingly endless expanse of hours until she can return to the familiar solace of her bed. a dissonant clang of the ticket machine shatters the silence and she starts as a bellhop-type from the nearby hotel emerges from the doorway his eyes squinting against the evening light. his voice a monotone recitation of 'can

She walks through the cracked sidewalk the creaky screen door slams shut behind her & the smell of stale air clings to her skin like a damp shroud. Memories rise up like dusty cobwebs her fingers tremble as she reaches for the rusty doorknob. The door swings open with a shriek of hinges like a scream in her ears. The foyer stretches out before her a labyrinth of memories the stairs creak beneath her feet like a chorus of restless spirits. The walls are the same faded pastel yellow the same cracks spreading like veins on a worn hand. She feels a presence hovering just out of reach like a whispered secret her skin prickling with gooseflesh. Is she alone or is she not? She can't quite recall. The air is heavy with the scent of decay & memories. In the kitchen the linoleum still bears the faint imprint of

she sits in the waiting room a lone figure hunched over a worn armchair her eyes fixed on the wall clock hands crawling 2:47 AM the fluorescent lights above hum a low vibration where is he she thinks a question that has been repeating in her mind for what feels like hours no answer comes she looks around the empty space the chairs arranged in a neat row like a parade of skeletons the vending machine beeps softly in the corner dispensing its mechanical song she gets up to buy a cup of coffee but then thinks better of it sits back down her legs heavy as lead the air is stale and thick with the scent of disinfectant she wraps her arms around herself trying to warm up the chill that's spread through her but her body remains cold as a stone what am I doing here she thinks

she stands in the laundry room of her apartment building, hands kneading the cold water out of a grey t-shirt like it's dough rising in the oven, but it's just fabric and water and soap her neighbor from downstairs he walks in, an older man with a kind face, and he nods hello, not really looking at her, but she nods back, her eyes fixed on the washing machine's slow, jerky rhythm her husband he's the one who usually does the laundry, but he's at work, or maybe not, maybe he's just avoiding her, it's hard to keep track of him anymore she separates the lights from the darks, a mundane task, but one that's always made her feel a little anxious, like she's trying to keep the world in order, but it's already too late, it's already unraveling, thread by thread she adds detergent, the cap comes off with

she stands on the corner of main and maple her eyes fixed on the bus stop a minute ago she was walking home on foot the weather was nice the sun beating down on her face now it's raining her feet get wet she doesn't care. the bus will be late it always is she checks her watch for what feels like the hundredth time 4:47. a car drives by honking its horn she doesn't even flinch. she thinks about the meeting earlier her boss was yelling at her for something she can't even remember. why did she even take this job. the money is okay but the stress is not worth it. a woman with a stroller walks by and smiles at her. she smiles back not really meaning to. the bus is late. 5:03. a gust of wind blows her hair back she pulls it up into a

dual_steered_high.text: Emily stood at the edge of the porch, her eyes widening in horror. Her hands clenched into fists, a scream locked in her throat. She stumbled forward, stumbling into the sunlight-drenched afternoon. She fell into the steps. A shattering. She rose. Her eyes were wet. Not a hair on her head. It was she. "Emily?" Emily said. Emily rose. Emily rose. I. She stumbled back. Stumbled back. Her fingers clawed. Clawed. The door. Emily stood before the door. Her fingers clawed. Clawed. It slid. Slid. Opened. Emily rose. Her eyes into eyes. They were on eyes. They were on Emily. "Emily?" Emily said. She found herself in a room. A room she knew. A room she had never left. The curtains hung the same, the curtains with the red flowers that seemed to dance in the afternoon sun. A chandelier hung over the dining table, casting a warm glow over

The fluorescent lights above flickered and hummed, casting an eerie glow on the walls. The clock on the wall read 3:47 AM. The waiting room was empty. A lone figure sat, motionless, in a chair. Her name was Lena. She wore a stained white dress, its fabric a deep crimson on the hem. Lena's face was ashen, her eyes sunken. Her mouth, once full, now a thin line. A vending machine, a distant coffee machine, and a broken clock formed a gruesome shrine to despair. The hospital's air reeked of disinfectant and stale air. The walls seemed to suffocate her. Outside, the city slept. Only the sounds of the city's nocturnal creatures – the distant hum of a train, the screech of a stray cat – broke the silence. The waiting room's lights seemed to mock her, as if they too knew the truth: that time stood still. She stared

It was a typical Monday morning. Emma stood in the laundry room of her apartment building, surrounded by the steaming sheets, the torn denim jeans, and the same flimsy sweater she'd been wearing yesterday. Her hands moved of their own accord, as if driven by a madness, as she stuffed a sock into the machine. The laundry room's fluorescent light flickered and dimmed, casting an eerie glow on the rows of mangled souls trapped in the spin cycle. It was a notorious struggle to keep her laundry clean, let alone alive. Emma checked the time. She'd left the building at exactly 6:00 AM, but her chance of leaving before the laundry finished seemed lost. She squinted at the clocks on the wall. Two hours and twenty minutes. She lit a cigarette, paced the room, checked the time, again. A gruff voice called out from behind her. "Hey, you're the one

As she stood on the corner, the fluorescent glow of the streetlight above flickered with each passing moment, Susan felt her eyes twitch. She was waiting for the bus. She had just come from her office, from a meeting, from her job. And she had just left. A single frame, cast forward in time. The bus was late. It had been 10 minutes, 20 minutes, 37 minutes since she had boarded the last one. The grey stone beneath her feet felt cold. She wore no shoes, because she had not expected to stand. The air clung heavy to her skin, the smell of exhaust and rain. It was not raining now. Not yet. She checked her watch. Again. The minute hand hung forward, a halting counter to the thoughts of a coming storm. The rain might not be enough to clear the roads, the storm drains already clogged, but it

Emily stood at the edge of the sidewalk, her eyes squinting in the bright sunlight. She felt a sudden flutter in her chest, a beat that was hers alone. She knew it was hers alone, yet. It was as if she had a heart outside her chest, beating and stuttering with each step, each breath. She took a step forward. She had to. She had not returned in twenty years. Not since the day she left, the day the world was torn from her grasp. And here she was, her footsteps echoing on the empty sidewalk, her heart racing as she turned the corner. The house stood before her, exactly as she had left it. Exactly. The paint was the same shade of cream, the shutters the same shade of blue. The porch creaked beneath her feet as she approached. She had almost forgotten the sound of it, almost. Almost.

The fluorescent lights above flickered and pulsed with a rhythmic, maddening beat, as if mocking the man's quiet desperation. He sat, ramrod straight, his back against the cold, gray wall, as if he would melt away if he shifted an inch. His eyes, two holes in his face, stared at the floor, where the lines converged, where the lines diverged, into an abyss of nothingness. The waiting room was a cavern of steel and vinyl, a cruel, unforgiving geometry that seemed to shrink with each passing minute. The air was heavy with the scent of antiseptic and stale coffee. A single, flickering fluorescent tube above the television cast an eerie glow on the vacant, foldable chairs, as if beckoning the shadows to come closer, to creep in, to take a seat. He sipped the cold coffee from the Styrofoam cup, its bitter taste a mockery of the hope that had

It was a typical Tuesday morning in the apartment building on 5th Street. The walls were a deep, unbroken mirror, and the windows, fogged, blurred the edges of the unbroken walls, as in a shattered glass. And I stood in the laundry room, which was untouched, so it was the only thing left untouched. I had been doing laundry in this room for as long as I could remember. It was a ritual, a thing I had grown accustomed to. Every Sunday, I came to this room, alone, with the clothes of my life. I was sorting, separating whites from colors, lights from darks. The washer, a worn, blue thing, with a cycle indicator that always, always, did not work, was humming, a low, consistent thrum. I had grown accustomed to this hum. It was the only sound I heard in this room, this silence, this stillness. The other tenants

As the fluorescent lights flickered out, Emma's eyes flickered open. The bus shelter stood empty, a perfect reflection of her face. She had been waiting for what felt like an eternity, her fingers tracing the ridges of the palm. The other commuters who stood beside her had walked away, turned into the passengers of another bus, a bus that had not yet appeared. The rain had stopped an hour ago, leaving the streets wet and dripping. The bus that had been late, was late. The bus that had been on time, was not. The bus that was coming, was not. The next bus arrived. It was late. Emma stepped out of the shelter and stepped into the bus. Emma looked at the space beside her, saw no one. She looked at the one across from her, saw no one. There was a boy with his eyes fixed on his phone.

Emily stood at the edge of a cliff, her eyes fixed on the town that had always been her home. She stared at the place she had fled, and yet, I was still. I was here. Emily stumbled through the dusty streets, her feet bare, her eyes wide with the truth. She is here. She stumbled upon a house, exactly as she had left it. The paint was chipped, the door creaked, the windows were boarded. Emily's eyes widened as she took a step closer. The door swung open. The creaking of the hinges sent shivers down her spine. Inside, the air was stale, the smell of decay, but also, the scent of her mother's perfume. Emily's eyes welled up with tears as she stepped inside. The rooms were exactly as she had left them. The kitchen, the living room, the bedroom, every single room was exactly as she had

The fluorescent lights above flickered and hummed, casting an otherworldly glow on the waiting room's walls. Emma sat alone, her eyes wide open, her fingers drumming a desperate rhythm on the armrest. The clock on the wall ticked on, each tick a countdown to the moment her world would shatter. She had been here for hours, or maybe days. Time lost all meaning in this forsaken place. The other patients, the ones who came and went, were just specters in her mind. They left behind only whispers, shadows, and an endless echo of pain. The hospital's artificial lighting cast an eerie glow on the windows, making the night outside seem even darker, even more treacherous. The city outside was alive, its sounds muffled by the sterile walls, but Emma felt the beat of its heart, the thrum of its energy. It was a cruel reminder that life went on, that

It was a Tuesday morning, and Emma stood in the laundry room of her apartment building, surrounded by rows of flickering fluorescent lights and whispers of a broken past. The detergent dispensers had been malfunctioning for weeks, but Emma was about to try and change that. She had been sending messages to the landlord, had been screaming at the laundry attendant who only laughed and winked at her, but today she had had enough. Emma had filled the washing machine with an entire box of Tide, overflowing from the box and pooling on the floor like a message from the dead. The load of whites hummed, shook, and rolled, as if trying to break free from the darkness that dwelled within. The night sweats of her neighbors, the spaghetti sauce of the Italian family on the third floor, the snippets of last night's Chinese takeout – all washed clean, all

As she stood on the corner, her eyes locked onto the arrivals board, the words changing in a blur of yesterday and tomorrow. Emily clutched her bus ticket, crumpled in her hand. Her bus ticket, crumpled, was an artifact of yesterday. The stop was 219. Her shift had ended six hours ago. She stood on the corner, four hours ago.
